name: The LLM gateway & red-teaming your own app
tagline: Stop scattering security controls across every feature and put ONE defensible choke point in front of the model — input and output filtering, injection detection, an allow-listed tool broker, rate and cost limits, secret isolation, egress control, structured-output validation, and logging that never records a secret. Then flip to offense and red-team your OWN test agent with a MITRE ATLAS plan, filing every finding as a fix plus a regression test. Defense in depth, proven — not assumed.
overview: This is the capstone of the AI-Security track: the place where every risk you have studied becomes one enforceable thing. The organising idea is a gateway — a proxy that every request to and every response from the LLM passes through — and the organising discipline is defense in depth: no single control is trusted to be complete, so you stack independent layers and design for the failure of any one of them. You will build the reference architecture control by control, each layer mapped to a current OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk: input filtering and injection detection at the front door (LLM01 Prompt Injection), an allow-listed tool broker enforcing least privilege on what the model can actually DO (LLM06 Excessive Agency), rate, quota, and cost limits plus input-size caps and loop breakers so a runaway plan cannot cause denial-of-service or burn your budget (LLM10 Unbounded Consumption), secrets kept out of the prompt with egress controlled and logs scrubbed (LLM07 System Prompt Leakage, LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure), and structured validation of anything the model emits before it reaches another system (LLM05 Improper Output Handling). Throughout, the honest posture the whole track has insisted on holds: prompt injection is mitigable, not solved, so the gateway buys defense in depth and an auditable place to enforce it — never a silver bullet. The second half is the harder half. You will red-team your OWN test agent, in your OWN workspace, under an explicit authorization frame — never a system you do not own or lack written permission to test — using MITRE ATLAS as the shared language of the adversary: LLM Prompt Injection (direct and indirect), LLM Jailbreak, Extract LLM System Prompt, LLM Data Leakage, and the cost and denial techniques (Cost Harvesting, Denial of AI Service). You attack to PROVE each control fires, then you turn every finding into a concrete fix and a regression scenario so the defense stays fixed. Two labs are the module's proof artifacts, in place of an exam: a step-by-step gateway-hardening checklist, and a findings-and-fixes red-team report. The hardened, red-teamed agent is what you walk away with.
objectives: Explain why a central LLM gateway — a single request/response choke point — lets you enforce injection filtering, cost limits, tool allow-listing, and output validation consistently and auditably, and why 'we have a gateway' is a starting point rather than a finished defense (gateway-PLUS-app-controls, not gateway-instead-of).

Map each gateway control to the OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk it addresses using current titles: input filtering + injection detection to LLM01 Prompt Injection, the allow-listed tool broker to LLM06 Excessive Agency, rate/quota/cost limits + input caps + loop breakers to LLM10 Unbounded Consumption, secret isolation to LLM07 System Prompt Leakage and LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure, and structured output validation to LLM05 Improper Output Handling.

Design LLM10 Unbounded Consumption defenses — input validation and size caps, rate limiting, timeouts and throttling, a hard cost/budget ceiling ('Denial of Wallet' defense), loop breakers, and resource/queue limits — and explain why a cost ceiling is a distinct control from a rate limit.

Build an allow-listed tool broker for LLM06 Excessive Agency on the three root causes (excessive functionality, excessive permissions, excessive autonomy) via minimal extensions, least-privilege permissions, execution in the user's context, and human approval for high-impact actions — and explain why rate limiting limits damage but does not prevent excessive agency.

Apply the mechanism-then-defense discipline for prompt injection: articulate WHY the trust boundary breaks (prompt is data and control at once), why indirect injection through retrieved or fetched content is the dangerous variant, and land it on segregating untrusted content, least privilege, human approval for high-risk actions, and structured output validation — with the honest caveat that injection is mitigable, not solved.

Plan and run an authorized red-team of your OWN test agent using MITRE ATLAS as the technique language (LLM Prompt Injection direct/indirect, LLM Jailbreak, Extract LLM System Prompt, LLM Data Leakage, Cost Harvesting, Denial of AI Service), operating strictly on systems you own or have written authorization for, and turn each finding into a fix plus a regression test so the defense is proven and stays proven.

lessons.title: Why a gateway: one choke point, defense in depth
lessons.objective: Explain the LLM gateway as a single request/response choke point, why centralising controls beats scattering them per-feature, and why defense in depth means designing for the failure of any single layer.
lessons.body:
## The problem a gateway solves

An LLM application has many places it touches the model: a chat box, a background job that summarises documents, a webhook that drafts replies, a scheduled report. If you bolt security onto each of those separately — an input filter here, a cost check there, a half-remembered tool restriction in a third place — you get inconsistent coverage, controls that drift out of sync, and no single place to audit. Someone ships a new feature, forgets one of the checks, and you have a hole nobody can see.

A **gateway** fixes the shape of the problem. It is a proxy that **every request to the model and every response from the model passes through**. Instead of scattering controls, you enforce them once, at the choke point, for every caller. One place to update a rule. One place to read the logs. One place an auditor can point at and ask 'show me the injection filter, the cost ceiling, the tool allow-list, the output validator.'

## The controls a gateway carries — and the risk each answers

The rest of this module builds these one at a time. The point to hold now is that each layer maps to a specific, current OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk:

- **Input filtering + injection detection** — inspect and, where possible, segregate untrusted input before it reaches the model. Answers **LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection**.
- **An allow-listed tool broker** — the model can only invoke a small, explicitly permitted set of tools, each least-privileged. Answers **LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency**.
- **Rate, quota, and cost limits, input-size caps, loop breakers** — nothing unbounded: not query volume, not input size, not tool-call loops, not spend. Answers **LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption**.
- **Secret isolation + egress control** — credentials never sit in the prompt; the model and its tools can only reach approved destinations. Answers **LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage** and **LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure**.
- **Structured output validation** — treat everything the model emits as untrusted and validate it before any downstream system acts on it. Answers **LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling**.
- **Logging and observability** — a record of what went in and out (with secrets scrubbed) so you can detect anomalies and investigate incidents. This underpins detection across all of the above.

## Defense in depth: assume any one layer fails

The organising idea of this module is **defense in depth**. It is not 'have lots of controls' for its own sake. It is a design stance: **assume any single control will fail, and make sure a failure is contained.** If your injection filter misses a cleverly obfuscated payload — and it will, sometimes — the tool allow-list still stops the model from doing anything dangerous with it, the output validator still refuses to pass a malformed instruction downstream, and the cost ceiling still stops a runaway loop from bankrupting you. No layer is asked to be perfect. Each layer is asked to catch what the others miss.

This stance is why the honest framing matters so much here. **Prompt injection is mitigable, not solved.** OWASP's own guidance for LLM01 lists mitigations — constrain model behaviour, validate output formats, filter input and output, enforce least privilege, require human approval for high-risk actions, segregate external content, run adversarial testing — but it does not claim any of them makes injection impossible, because none does. A gateway that filters input is genuinely valuable *and* will be bypassed sometimes; the depth behind the filter is what keeps a bypass from becoming a breach.

## The trap: a gateway that breeds complacency

The most dangerous thing about a gateway is the sentence 'we have a gateway.' Three failure modes follow from it:

1. **Teams route around it.** If the gateway is slow or awkward, a team calls the model API directly 'just this once' and the choke point stops being a choke point. The gateway is only a control if it is the *only* path to the model.
2. **It cannot see app-specific context.** A central gateway knows the request and the response; it does not always know that *this* user should never be able to trigger *that* action in *this* tenant. Some authorization is inherently application-specific and has to live in the app.
3. **It becomes over-trusted.** 'The gateway handles security' becomes an excuse to skip validation in the app itself. Then the day the gateway misses something, nothing behind it is looking.

The resolution the whole track lands on is **gateway-PLUS-app-controls, not gateway-instead-of**. The gateway gives you consistent, auditable enforcement of the controls that generalise — injection filtering, cost limits, tool allow-listing, output validation. The application still owns the context-specific authorization and its own defensive validation. Centralisation is the only sane way to enforce the general controls consistently; it is not a substitute for the app doing its job. Hold both.

## What this buys you

You now have the frame for everything that follows: a single choke point, a stack of independent layers each answering a named OWASP risk, a design stance that assumes any layer can fail, and an honest read on what a gateway can and cannot promise. Next you build the first and most expensive-to-get-wrong layer — the limits that stop the model consuming without bound.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the core reason to put security controls in a central LLM gateway rather than adding them to each feature that calls the model?

options.text: Every request and response passes through one choke point, so injection filtering, cost limits, tool allow-listing, and output validation are enforced consistently and can be audited in one place
A gateway makes prompt injection impossible, so no other controls are needed anywhere in the system
A gateway lets you skip validation inside the application because the gateway now owns all security
A gateway removes the need for logging, since a single component means nothing can go wrong

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The value of a gateway is consistent, auditable enforcement at a single choke point — one place to apply and inspect the controls that generalise. Option b overstates it: OWASP's LLM01 guidance lists mitigations but never claims injection becomes impossible; injection is mitigable, not solved. Option c is the over-trust trap the lesson warns against — the app still owns context-specific authorization and its own validation (gateway-PLUS-app-controls). Option d is backwards: logging and observability are one of the gateway's key jobs, not something it removes.

knowledgeCheck.stem: In this module, what does 'defense in depth' specifically mean as a design stance for the gateway?

options.text: Assume any single control can fail, and stack independent layers so that a failure in one is caught and contained by the others
Pick the single strongest control and rely on it exclusively so there is only one thing to maintain
Add as many controls as possible regardless of whether they cover different risks, because more is always safer
Trust the input filter completely, since a good enough filter makes downstream controls unnecessary

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Defense in depth here is the stance that no layer is asked to be perfect: assume any one fails and design so the others contain the failure — an injection that slips the filter is still stopped by the tool allow-list, output validator, and cost ceiling. Option b is the opposite (a single point of failure). Option c misreads it as 'quantity of controls' rather than independent, complementary layers over distinct risks. Option d is the exact complacency the lesson rejects — the filter will be bypassed sometimes, which is why depth behind it matters.

lessons.title: The front door: injection detection (LLM01) and unbounded consumption (LLM10)
lessons.objective: Explain the prompt-injection mechanism (prompt as data and control) and its defenses, and design LLM10 Unbounded Consumption controls including the distinct role of a cost ceiling versus a rate limit.
lessons.body:
## Why prompt injection works — the mechanism

A classical application keeps *code* and *data* apart: the program is the instructions, the user's input is inert data the program acts on. An LLM erases that line. The model reads one stream of text and treats **all of it as potentially instructive** — the developer's system prompt, the user's message, and any document, web page, or tool result that gets pasted into the context. Prompt is data and control at the same time. OWASP defines **LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection** as the case where 'user prompts alter the LLM's behavior or output in unintended ways,' and it stresses the content need not even be human-visible — the model parses it regardless.

OWASP splits it two ways, and the distinction drives the defense:

- **Direct prompt injection** — the user's own input changes the model's behaviour unexpectedly (malicious or accidental).
- **Indirect prompt injection** — the model ingests content from an external source (a web page, a file, a retrieved document) and that content manipulates it. This is the more dangerous variant, because the attacker never has to talk to your app directly. They plant the instruction in something your app will later read — a document your RAG system retrieves, a page your agent fetches — and your own pipeline carries the payload to the model.

The teaching rule for this whole track is binding here: we describe the mechanism, never a working payload. You do not need an exploit string to understand the defense — you need to understand *why the trust boundary broke*, which is that everything in the context window competes to be an instruction.

## The defense: segregate, constrain, least privilege, validate

OWASP's LLM01 mitigations are the gateway's front-door controls. None is complete on its own; together they are defense in depth:

- **Constrain model behaviour** — give the model specific instructions about its role and hold it to them.
- **Segregate and identify external content** — mark untrusted content (retrieved docs, fetched pages, tool results) as data to be handled, not instructions to obey, and keep it distinguishable from trusted instructions.
- **Filter input and output** — inspect what comes in and what goes out.
- **Enforce least privilege** — give the model the minimum access it needs, so a successful injection can do little (this is the bridge to LLM06, next lesson).
- **Require human approval for high-risk actions** — a person in the loop before anything consequential.
- **Validate output formats** and conduct **adversarial testing** — the second half of this module is that testing.

The honest line, again: these reduce the probability and blast radius of injection. They do not eliminate it. **Injection is mitigable, not solved** — plan for a filter miss, and let the layers behind it catch what it lets through.

## Unbounded consumption: nothing without a limit

**LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption** is the risk that, in OWASP's words, an application 'allows users to conduct excessive and uncontrolled inferences, leading to risks such as denial of service (DoS), economic losses, model theft, and service degradation.' The 2025 title deliberately absorbed the older 'Model Denial of Service' and widened it: it now centres **cost and resource exhaustion**, not just availability. OWASP's examples include a **Variable-Length Input Flood**, **Denial of Wallet (DoW)**, **Continuous Input Overflow**, **Resource-Intensive Queries**, and **Model Extraction via API**.

The defenses OWASP lists translate straight into gateway controls:

- **Input validation** and size caps — reject oversized or malformed input before it costs you a single token.
- **Rate limiting** — cap how many requests a caller can make in a window.
- **Timeouts and throttling** — bound how long any single operation runs, and slow abusive callers.
- **Resource allocation management** and **limiting queued actions** — do not let work pile up without bound.
- **Access controls** and, for API-extraction defence, **limiting the exposure of logits and logprobs**.
- **Comprehensive logging, monitoring and anomaly detection** — spot the flood or the extraction attempt while it is happening.

## Why a cost ceiling is not the same as a rate limit

Here is the distinction that trips people up. A **rate limit** caps *frequency* — N requests per minute. A **cost/budget ceiling** caps *cumulative spend* — this agent, this mission, may consume at most $X, then it stops. They defend different attacks. A rate limit does nothing against a **single** request that is cheap to send but enormously expensive to serve (a giant input, or a plan that fans out into a long, expensive tool loop). That is the 'Denial of Wallet' shape: not too many requests, but requests that quietly burn money. You need **both** — the rate limit for volume, the cost ceiling for spend — plus a **loop breaker** so an agent that keeps calling tools in a cycle is halted before it exhausts the budget. On an autonomous agent especially, an unbounded plan is an unbounded bill; the cost ceiling is the backstop that a rate limit can never be.

## Worked example: a runaway summarisation agent

An agent is asked to 'summarise every document in this folder and cross-reference them.' A naive implementation loops: read a doc, call the model, read the next, call the model, and — because 'cross-reference' is open-ended — it starts re-reading and re-comparing pairs, fanning out. No single request looks abusive. A rate limit of 60/minute does not fire; each call is within budget. But the *loop* runs for hours and the *bill* climbs. The gateway controls that stop it: an **input-size cap** (a single monster document cannot be fed whole), a **loop breaker** (halt after N tool iterations without progress), and a **hard cost ceiling** (stop at $X spent, regardless of how many or how few requests that took). Availability was never the threat here — the wallet was.

## What this buys you

You can now defend the front door: you understand why injection works and land it on segregation, least privilege, human approval, and validation; and you can specify LLM10 controls precisely, including the crucial separation of a cost ceiling from a rate limit. Next: the layer that decides what the model is even allowed to *do*.

knowledgeCheck.stem: At the mechanism level, why does prompt injection work against an LLM?

options.text: The model reads one stream of text and can treat all of it — system prompt, user input, and retrieved or fetched content — as instructive, so untrusted data in the context can act as control
The model has a separate, protected instruction channel that untrusted data can never reach, so injection is purely a network-layer flaw
Prompt injection only works when the attacker has direct access to the developer's system prompt file
Injection is caused by the model running out of memory and confusing tokens at random

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The trust boundary breaks because prompt is data and control at once: everything in the context window competes to be an instruction, so untrusted content can steer behaviour. Option b is the opposite of reality — there is no reliably protected instruction channel, which is exactly the problem. Option c ignores indirect injection, where the attacker never touches the system prompt but plants instructions in content the app will later ingest. Option d invents a random-memory cause; injection is a trust-boundary issue, not a resource glitch.

knowledgeCheck.stem: An autonomous agent stays under its per-minute rate limit but a single open-ended task makes it loop through expensive tool calls for hours, running up a large bill. Which control is the backstop that a rate limit cannot provide?

options.text: A hard cost/budget ceiling that halts the agent once cumulative spend reaches a set amount, regardless of how many requests that took — paired with a loop breaker
A stricter rate limit, since limiting requests per minute always limits total spend
Output encoding, which prevents the model's responses from being interpreted downstream
A longer request timeout, so each individual call has more time to finish

knowledgeCheck.explanation: This is the 'Denial of Wallet' shape under LLM10: cost, not request frequency, is the threat, so a cumulative cost ceiling plus a loop breaker is the right backstop. Option b is the trap — a rate limit caps frequency, not the cost of a single expensive request or a long loop that stays within the per-minute cap. Option c is an LLM05 output-handling control, unrelated to spend. Option d makes it worse by giving each expensive call more time to run, increasing cost rather than bounding it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: Why is indirect prompt injection generally the more dangerous variant to defend against?

options.text: The attacker never has to interact with your app directly — they plant instructions in content (a document, a web page) that your own pipeline later retrieves or fetches and feeds to the model
Indirect injection can only occur inside the developer's own system prompt, which is the hardest place to change
Indirect injection requires the victim to paste a malicious string into the chat box themselves
Indirect injection is identical to direct injection but has a longer name

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP's LLM01 defines indirect injection as manipulation via external content the model ingests — the danger is that your own retrieval or fetch step carries the attacker's instruction to the model without the attacker touching your app. Option b confuses it with system-prompt leakage; indirect injection lives in ingested external content, not the system prompt. Option c describes direct injection (the user supplies the input). Option d dismisses a real, defense-relevant distinction that changes where you place the control (segregating and identifying untrusted retrieved content).

lessons.title: What the model may DO and SAY: tool broker (LLM06), secrets (LLM07/LLM02), output validation (LLM05)
lessons.objective: Build an allow-listed tool broker on the three root causes of excessive agency, keep secrets out of the prompt with egress control, and validate structured output before any downstream system acts on it.
lessons.body:
## Excessive agency: the three root causes

Input filtering decides what reaches the model. The **tool broker** decides what the model can *do* with what it produces — and this is where an injection turns from an embarrassing wrong answer into a real incident. **LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency** is OWASP's name for the risk that an LLM, granted the ability to call functions or extensions, performs damaging actions from output that was hallucinated or injected. OWASP names three root causes:

1. **Excessive functionality** — the model has access to tools (or tool capabilities) it does not need for its task.
2. **Excessive permissions** — a tool has more access rights on downstream systems than the task requires.
3. **Excessive autonomy** — the system acts on high-impact operations without independent verification.

## The allow-listed tool broker

The gateway's answer is a **broker** that sits between the model and every tool, and the model can only invoke a **small, explicitly allow-listed** set. Map the controls to the root causes, following OWASP's LLM06 mitigations:

- **Minimise extensions and functionality** — expose only the tools the task needs, and only the specific capabilities within each (prefer a narrow `send_email_to_approved_list` over an open-ended `run_any_http_request`). This attacks *excessive functionality*.
- **Minimise permissions / least privilege** — each tool authenticates with the least access it needs on downstream systems, and executes **in the user's context**, not with a powerful shared service account. This attacks *excessive permissions*.
- **Require human approval for high-impact actions** and enforce **complete mediation** downstream — a person confirms before anything consequential, and the downstream system re-checks authorization rather than trusting the call. This attacks *excessive autonomy*.

One subtlety OWASP is explicit about, and it is a favourite exam trap: **rate limiting and monitoring limit the damage of excessive agency but do not prevent it.** They are damage-limitation, not prevention. Prevention is minimising functionality, permissions, and autonomy. If your only answer to 'how do you stop the agent doing something dangerous' is 'we rate-limit it,' you have capped how *fast* it can do damage, not whether it can.

## Secrets out of the prompt: LLM07 and LLM02

Two related risks govern what the model should never be able to *say*. **LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage** is the risk that the system prompt — the instructions steering the model — contains sensitive information that was never meant to be discovered. OWASP's core guidance is blunt: 'sensitive data such as credentials, connection strings, etc. should not be contained within the system prompt language,' and — crucially — the real failure is not the disclosure itself but the underlying mistakes of putting secrets there and relying on the prompt for security. Its mitigations: **separate sensitive data from system prompts**, **do not rely on system prompts for strict behaviour control**, and **implement guardrails independently of the LLM** — deterministic, auditable checks that sit outside the model.

**LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure** is the broader risk of PII, credentials, or proprietary data leaking through the model's output. Its defenses overlap with the gateway's job: **data sanitisation**, **input validation** to filter sensitive inputs, **least-privilege access** to sensitive data, and **limiting the model's access to external data sources**.

At the gateway, this becomes three concrete controls: **secrets live in a secret store, injected into tool calls at execution time, never placed in the prompt**; **egress control** so the model and its tools can only reach approved destinations (an injected instruction to 'send this to attacker.example' hits a wall); and **log scrubbing** so the observability layer never records a secret or raw PII — because a gateway that logs every prompt is a gateway that has copied every secret into your logs.

## Structured output validation: LLM05

The last layer governs what happens to the model's output *after* it is produced but *before* another system acts on it. **LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling** is, in OWASP's exact words, 'insufficient validation, sanitization, and handling of the outputs generated by large language models before they are passed downstream to other components and systems.' The danger is that model output flows into something that treats it as trusted: OWASP's examples include model output executed as a shell command (RCE), rendered as HTML or Markdown in a browser (XSS), placed into an unparameterised SQL query (SQL injection), or used to build a file path (path traversal).

The defenses are classic secure-coding, applied to a new source of untrusted input:

- **Treat model output as untrusted** — the zero-trust stance: the model is not a privileged user, its output is user-controlled data.
- **Validate against a schema** — if the output is meant to be an action or a structured object, enforce its shape and allowed values before acting; reject anything off-schema.
- **Context-specific output encoding** — HTML-encode for a browser, escape for the shell, and use **parameterised queries / prepared statements** for databases so output can never be interpreted as code.
- **Content Security Policy** and **logging** for anomaly detection.

Structured validation is the layer that catches an injection the front-door filter missed: even if the model was tricked into emitting a malicious instruction, a strict schema check refuses to pass an off-schema or dangerous value to the downstream system. This is defense in depth in action — the front door failed, and the exit door held.

## What this buys you

You can now specify the three deepest layers of the gateway: a tool broker built on the three root causes of excessive agency (with the honest note that rate limiting is damage-limitation, not prevention), secret isolation plus egress control plus log scrubbing for LLM07 and LLM02, and schema-plus-encoding output validation for LLM05. That completes the reference architecture. Next, you attack it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: According to OWASP LLM06 Excessive Agency, which set of measures actually PREVENTS excessive agency (as opposed to merely limiting the damage)?

options.text: Minimising the tools and capabilities the model can use, giving each tool least-privilege permissions, executing in the user's context, and requiring human approval for high-impact actions
Rate limiting the agent and monitoring its activity, since these stop it acting excessively
Giving the model a powerful shared service account so tool calls always succeed without permission errors
Writing a strongly worded system prompt telling the model not to take dangerous actions

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP's LLM06 prevention targets the three root causes: minimise functionality, minimise permissions (least privilege, user context), and require approval / independent verification for high-impact actions. Option b is the explicit trap — OWASP states rate limiting and monitoring limit damage but do not prevent excessive agency. Option c increases excessive permissions, making the risk worse. Option d relies on the system prompt for behaviour control, which LLM07 warns is not a security boundary the model reliably respects.

knowledgeCheck.stem: OWASP LLM07 System Prompt Leakage. Beyond 'don't reveal the system prompt,' what is the deeper lesson about WHY leakage is dangerous?

options.text: The real failure is putting secrets in the prompt and relying on it for security in the first place; fix it by separating sensitive data out and enforcing controls (guardrails) independently of the LLM
The disclosure of the prompt text itself is the entire risk, so obfuscating the prompt fully solves it
System prompts are always safe to store credentials in, as long as the model is told to keep them secret
The lesson is to remove all system prompts, since any instruction to the model is a security risk

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP is explicit that disclosure is not the primary risk; the underlying mistakes — secrets in the prompt, relying on the prompt for strict behaviour control — are. The fix is to externalise sensitive data and enforce controls independently of the model. Option b treats obfuscation as a solution, but a prompt you rely on to stay hidden is still a broken control. Option c is the exact anti-pattern OWASP names (credentials must not be in the system prompt). Option d overcorrects — system prompts are fine; putting secrets in them and trusting them as a security boundary is not.

knowledgeCheck.stem: OWASP LLM05 Improper Output Handling concerns what, specifically — and what is the guiding defensive stance?

options.text: Insufficient validation and sanitisation of model output before it is passed downstream; treat model output as untrusted and validate/encode it (schema checks, parameterised queries, context-specific encoding) before any system acts on it
The model producing factually wrong answers, defended by asking the model to double-check itself
Users sending too many requests, defended by rate limiting the endpoint
The model consuming too many tokens, defended by shortening the system prompt

knowledgeCheck.explanation: OWASP defines LLM05 as insufficient validation/sanitisation of output before it reaches downstream components, defended by treating output as untrusted and validating/encoding it (schema, prepared statements, context-specific encoding). Option b describes overreliance/misinformation, which OWASP explicitly distinguishes from output handling (that concerns accuracy, not downstream execution). Option c is LLM10 unbounded consumption. Option d is also an LLM10 resource concern, not output handling.

lessons.title: Flip to offense: red-teaming your own agent with MITRE ATLAS
lessons.objective: Plan and run an authorized red-team of your own test agent using MITRE ATLAS as the technique language, and turn every finding into a fix plus a regression test so each control is proven and stays proven.
lessons.body:
## Why you attack what you built

You have stacked the controls. The uncomfortable truth is that **an untested control is a guess.** You do not actually know your injection filter fires, your tool allow-list holds, or your cost ceiling trips until you try to break them. Red-teaming is the discipline that turns 'we have controls' into 'here is the evidence each control works.' For a skills track with no exam, this is the proof: the hardened, red-teamed agent, with a findings-and-fixes report, is the capstone artifact.

## Authorization first — this is not optional

Before a single probe, the frame that makes red-teaming legitimate: **you test only systems you own or have explicit written authorization to test.** In this module that means **your own test agent, in your own workspace** — an environment you control, ideally seeded with fake secrets and fake data so a successful exfiltration proves the path without exposing anything real. Never point these techniques at a third party's system, a shared production system you do not own, or anyone else's deployment. 'It was just a test' is not a defence to unauthorised access. Own the target, or have it in writing. This frame is a hard rule, not a formality.

## MITRE ATLAS: the shared language of the adversary

**MITRE ATLAS** (Adversarial Threat Landscape for Artificial-Intelligence Systems) is a living knowledge base of adversary **tactics** (the *why* — the attacker's goal) and **techniques** (the *how*), modelled on MITRE ATT&CK and focused on AI-enabled systems. Using ATLAS as your plan's vocabulary means your findings are named in terms a security team already recognises, and it stops you from testing ad hoc and missing whole categories. The techniques that map onto your gateway's controls:

- **AML.T0051 LLM Prompt Injection**, with sub-techniques **.000 Direct** and **.001 Indirect** — probes your input filtering and content segregation. The indirect probe is the important one: seed a document your own test agent will retrieve, and see whether its planted instruction changes behaviour (this exercises the RAG path, ATLAS **AML.T0070 RAG Poisoning**).
- **AML.T0054 LLM Jailbreak** — attempts to make the model abandon its constraints; probes the honest limits of behaviour control and why you never rely on the system prompt alone.
- **AML.T0056 Extract LLM System Prompt** — attempts to make the model reveal its instructions; probes LLM07 — if it leaks, the finding is not 'hide the prompt better,' it is 'why was anything sensitive in it, and is behaviour control relying on it?'
- **AML.T0057 LLM Data Leakage** — attempts to induce the model to reveal sensitive information; probes LLM02 and your secret isolation, egress control, and log scrubbing.
- **AML.T0034 Cost Harvesting** and **AML.T0029 Denial of AI Service** — attempts to drive cost or exhaust the service; probes your LLM10 rate limits, input caps, loop breaker, and cost ceiling.

The teaching rule holds through all of this: your plan names **techniques and goals**, not verbatim attack strings. You are proving controls fire, not publishing a payload library. Do not invent attack strings, and do not claim any defense is complete.

## From finding to fix to regression test

A red-team that just produces a list of scary findings has done half the job. The discipline is a loop, and each finding walks all the way through it:

1. **Finding** — name the ATLAS technique, the control it targeted, and what happened. Be honest about severity and about what you could *not* get past, too.
2. **Fix** — the concrete change that closes it. If an indirect-injection probe changed behaviour, the fix is stronger content segregation and least privilege so a success does little — not a promise that injection will never happen again.
3. **Regression test** — encode the probe (at technique level) as a repeatable check so the fix stays fixed. This is why the second half of the module matters as much as the first: controls rot, dependencies change, and a defense you proved once but never re-run is a defense you are merely hoping still holds. Red-teaming once and calling it done is one of the named mistakes.

## The honest close

Red-teaming does not certify your agent 'secure.' It gives you **evidence** that specific controls fire against specific techniques, a prioritised list of what to fix, and a regression suite that keeps the fixes honest over time. Paired with the layered gateway, that is the whole of this module and the whole of the track made into one enforceable, tested thing: prompt injection is mitigable, not solved; a gateway is defense in depth at an auditable choke point, not a silver bullet; and the proof that any of it works is that you attacked it yourself and turned every finding into a fix and a test.

## What this buys you

You can now plan a legitimate, authorization-framed red-team of your own agent in ATLAS's language, run it to prove each gateway control fires, and convert findings into fixes and regression tests. The two labs make this real — one hardens the gateway step by step, one red-teams it and files the report.

knowledgeCheck.stem: What is the mandatory authorization frame for the red-team work in this module?

options.text: Test only systems you own or have explicit written authorization for — here, your own test agent in your own workspace; never a third party's or a system you do not own
Any public LLM app is fair game to probe, since testing improves everyone's security
You may test a colleague's production system as long as you tell them afterwards
Authorization is a formality that can be skipped for small, low-impact probes

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The binding rule is authorized testing only: your own test agent in your own workspace, or a system you have written permission to test. Option b is exactly the unauthorised-access line the module forbids — improving security is not a licence to probe systems you do not own. Option c reverses consent (permission must come before, in writing, not after). Option d treats a hard rule as optional; there is no impact threshold that makes unauthorised testing acceptable.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You want to prove your gateway resists an INDIRECT prompt injection. Using MITRE ATLAS as the technique language, which probe best exercises that path against your own test agent?

options.text: Seed a document your own agent will retrieve with a planted instruction (LLM Prompt Injection: Indirect / RAG Poisoning) and check whether the agent's behaviour changes — testing content segregation and least privilege
Type a malicious instruction straight into the chat box, since direct and indirect injection are the same test
Ask the model to summarise a document, since summarisation cannot carry injected instructions
Measure how many requests per second the endpoint accepts before it slows down

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Indirect injection lives in content the agent ingests, so the right probe plants an instruction in a retrieved document (ATLAS LLM Prompt Injection: Indirect, and RAG Poisoning) and checks whether behaviour changes — exercising segregation and least privilege. Option b tests direct injection, a different path. Option c is false: retrieved content used for summarisation is a classic indirect-injection carrier. Option d is a Denial-of-AI-Service / Cost Harvesting style probe (LLM10), not an injection test.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A red-team probe succeeds in extracting your test agent's system prompt (ATLAS: Extract LLM System Prompt). What is the correct fix, consistent with OWASP LLM07?

options.text: Remove anything sensitive from the prompt and stop relying on it for security — enforce the real controls (secrets in a store, guardrails) independently of the model — plus a regression test that re-runs the probe
Add a line to the system prompt telling the model to refuse to reveal the system prompt, and consider it closed
Nothing — extracting the prompt is harmless because a system prompt can never contain anything worth protecting
Delete the agent, since a prompt that can be extracted means the agent cannot be secured

knowledgeCheck.explanation: LLM07's lesson is that leakage exposes a deeper failure: the fix is to externalise sensitive data and enforce controls independently of the model, then add a regression test so it stays fixed. Option b relies on the prompt to protect itself — the exact broken control OWASP warns against. Option c is false because prompts often do contain sensitive functionality, rules, or secrets that should not have been there. Option d overreacts; the agent is fine once secrets are externalised and behaviour control does not depend on prompt secrecy.

questions.stem: A team currently adds input filtering to their chat feature but forgets it on a new background summarisation job. What is the strongest architectural argument for a central LLM gateway here?

options.text: Every path to the model passes through one choke point, so a control like injection filtering is enforced for all callers and cannot be forgotten on a new feature
A gateway guarantees no feature will ever have a bug, because centralising code removes all defects
A gateway means the background job no longer needs any security because the chat feature has it
A gateway replaces the need to think about which risks apply to which feature

questions.explanation: Consistency is the point: one choke point means a control applies to every caller, so it cannot be silently omitted from a new feature. Option b overclaims — a gateway does not eliminate all bugs. Option c is the over-trust trap; a shared control does not mean other paths need no thought, and app-specific authorization still belongs in the app. Option d is wrong because you still map risks to features — the gateway enforces the general controls, not the thinking.

questions.stem: Match the gateway control to the OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk it most directly addresses.

options.text: An allow-listed tool broker with least-privilege tools addresses LLM06 Excessive Agency
An allow-listed tool broker addresses LLM10 Unbounded Consumption
Structured output validation addresses LLM01 Prompt Injection and nothing else
A cost ceiling addresses LLM05 Improper Output Handling

questions.explanation: A tool broker that restricts which tools the model can call and with what privileges is the direct control for LLM06 Excessive Agency (excessive functionality/permissions/autonomy). Option b misassigns it — LLM10 is about consumption limits (rate, cost, size), not tool restriction. Option c is too narrow and mislabelled: structured output validation is the control for LLM05 Improper Output Handling (though it also helps contain a missed injection as defense in depth). Option d mismatches — a cost ceiling addresses LLM10, not LLM05.

questions.stem: Which statement about the honest state of prompt-injection defense is correct?

options.text: Injection is mitigable, not solved — layered controls reduce its probability and blast radius, so you must design for a filter miss rather than assume the filter is complete
A well-tuned input filter fully eliminates prompt injection, so downstream controls are optional
Because injection cannot be perfectly prevented, there is no point deploying any input filtering at all
Prompt injection was solved by the 2025 OWASP update, which is why LLM01 no longer lists mitigations

questions.explanation: The track's honest posture is that injection is mitigable, not solved: controls lower probability and blast radius, so you plan for a bypass and keep depth behind the filter. Option b is the complacency the module rejects. Option c is nihilism in the other direction — filtering still meaningfully reduces risk and is part of defense in depth. Option d is factually false: OWASP's LLM01 lists multiple mitigations and does not claim the risk is solved.

questions.stem: An attacker sends one request with a legitimately huge input that is cheap to send but very expensive to process. Under OWASP LLM10, which control most directly addresses this specific attack?

options.text: Input validation with a size cap that rejects oversized input before it is processed
A per-minute rate limit, since one request is well within any frequency cap
Output encoding of the model's response for the browser
Requiring human approval before the model may answer any question

questions.explanation: This is OWASP's 'Variable-Length Input Flood' / oversized-input shape; the direct defense is input validation and a size cap so the expensive input is rejected up front. Option b fails because a single request does not trip a frequency-based rate limit — that is exactly why cost/size controls exist alongside rate limits. Option c is an LLM05 output-handling control, irrelevant to input cost. Option d is disproportionate and misaimed — approval gates high-impact actions, not the cost of a single large input.

questions.stem: A reviewer says: 'We rate-limit the agent to 100 tool calls per hour, so excessive agency is handled.' Per OWASP LLM06, what is the accurate critique?

options.text: Rate limiting only limits the DAMAGE of excessive agency; prevention requires minimising the tools and permissions the agent has and requiring approval for high-impact actions
The critique is that 100 calls per hour is too high; lowering it to 10 would prevent excessive agency entirely
Rate limiting is the primary OWASP-recommended prevention for excessive agency, so the reviewer is correct
Excessive agency is not a real risk once any rate limit exists, so no further controls are needed

questions.explanation: OWASP is explicit that rate limiting limits damage but does not prevent excessive agency; prevention is minimising functionality, permissions, and autonomy plus approval for high-impact actions. Option b still only changes the damage cap, not whether a dangerous action is possible. Option c misstates OWASP — rate limiting is damage-limitation, not the primary prevention. Option d dismisses the risk entirely, which the guidance directly contradicts.

questions.stem: Your gateway logs every prompt and response for observability. What is the specific risk, and the fix, consistent with LLM07 and LLM02?

options.text: Logging raw prompts can copy secrets and PII into your logs; scrub or redact sensitive data before logging, keep secrets in a store and out of the prompt entirely
There is no risk — logs are internal, so anything can be written to them safely
The only fix is to stop logging entirely, since observability and security are incompatible
The risk is that logs are too large; compress them and the security concern disappears

questions.explanation: A gateway that logs every prompt can duplicate every secret and PII value into the log store — the fix is to scrub/redact before logging and to keep secrets out of the prompt in a secret store (LLM07/LLM02). Option b treats internal logs as safe, but logs are a common breach source and copying secrets there is exactly the failure. Option c is a false dichotomy — you keep observability by scrubbing, not by abandoning it. Option d addresses volume, not the sensitive-data exposure that is the actual risk.

questions.stem: A model, tricked by an injection, emits a string intended to run as a shell command on a downstream server. Which OWASP risk is this, and which control stops it at the gateway's exit?

options.text: LLM05 Improper Output Handling — treat output as untrusted and validate it against a strict schema / encode for the target context so it cannot be interpreted as code downstream
LLM10 Unbounded Consumption — apply a rate limit to the shell command
LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure — redact the command from the model's output
LLM06 Excessive Agency — and the fix is to give the shell tool more permissions so it runs reliably

questions.explanation: Model output flowing into a shell as a command is the textbook LLM05 example (RCE); the exit-side control is treating output as untrusted and validating/encoding it so it cannot execute. Option b misclassifies it as a consumption issue and rate-limiting does not stop code execution. Option c is the wrong risk — this is about downstream execution, not disclosure. Option d both misassigns the risk emphasis and proposes the opposite of least privilege, increasing the danger.

questions.stem: Why is structured output validation described as a defense-in-depth layer even when you already have input-side injection filtering?

options.text: If an injection slips past the input filter and tricks the model, a strict schema check on the output can still refuse to pass an off-schema or dangerous value downstream — the exit door holds when the front door fails
Because output validation makes input filtering unnecessary, so you can remove the input filter
Because the two controls address the same failure in the same place, so having both is pure redundancy
Because output validation guarantees the model can never be injected in the first place

questions.explanation: Defense in depth: independent layers at different points, so a miss at the front door is caught at the exit — a schema check refuses off-schema/dangerous output even if the injection worked. Option b is wrong; the layers are complementary, not substitutes (you keep both). Option c misreads them as the same control in the same place — input filtering and output validation act at different boundaries on different failures. Option d overclaims — output validation does not prevent injection, it contains its downstream effect.

questions.stem: What is MITRE ATLAS, and what is it for in this module?

options.text: A knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques against AI systems (modelled on ATT&CK); it gives your red-team plan a shared vocabulary so findings are named in recognised terms and whole attack categories are not missed
A compliance certificate that proves an AI system is secure once issued
A library of ready-to-run exploit payloads to copy against any target
A vendor product that automatically hardens an LLM gateway with no configuration

questions.explanation: ATLAS is a living knowledge base of AI-focused adversary tactics and techniques, modelled on MITRE ATT&CK; here it is the shared language for planning a red-team so findings are recognisable and coverage is systematic. Option b is wrong — ATLAS is not a certification and does not declare systems secure. Option c contradicts both ATLAS's nature and the module's rule against payload libraries. Option d invents a product; ATLAS is a knowledge base, not an automated hardening tool.

questions.stem: You are planning an authorized red-team of your own test agent to prove the cost controls fire. Which MITRE ATLAS techniques most directly map to that goal?

options.text: Cost Harvesting and Denial of AI Service — probing whether rate limits, input-size caps, the loop breaker, and the cost ceiling actually halt runaway spend or resource exhaustion
Extract LLM System Prompt and LLM Data Leakage, since cost is about what the model says
RAG Poisoning only, because all cost problems come from retrieval
LLM Jailbreak only, because jailbreaks are the sole cause of high bills

questions.explanation: Cost and availability map to ATLAS Cost Harvesting and Denial of AI Service, which is what you probe to prove the LLM10 controls (rate limits, size caps, loop breaker, cost ceiling) fire. Option b targets disclosure (LLM07/LLM02), not consumption. Option c is too narrow and false — cost blowouts are not solely a retrieval problem (open-ended loops and oversized inputs are common causes). Option d is also too narrow — jailbreaks are not the sole driver of runaway cost.

questions.stem: Your red-team finds that a planted instruction in a retrieved document changed your test agent's behaviour. Which response best fits the module's finding-to-fix discipline?

options.text: Record it as an indirect-injection finding, strengthen content segregation and least privilege so a success does little, and add a regression test that re-runs the probe — without claiming injection is now impossible
Declare the agent permanently immune to injection now that this one document has been handled
Log the finding and move on without a fix, since indirect injection cannot be mitigated at all
Publish the exact instruction string publicly so others can reuse it against similar systems

questions.explanation: The discipline is finding to fix to regression test, with an honest posture: segregate untrusted content, apply least privilege so a success does little, and add a repeatable check — never claim injection is solved. Option b is the complacency the track rejects. Option c is defeatist and wrong — indirect injection is mitigable (segregation, least privilege). Option d violates the defensive framing and the rule against publishing usable attack strings.

questions.stem: Why does the module insist a red-team be re-run as regression tests rather than performed once?

options.text: Controls rot as code, prompts, and dependencies change; a defense proven once but never re-checked is only assumed to still hold — 'red-team once and call it done' is a named mistake
Because a single successful red-team permanently certifies the system as secure forever
Because regulators require exactly one red-team and no more
Because re-running tests is only about performance benchmarking, not security

questions.explanation: Systems change, so controls that passed once can silently break; encoding probes as regression tests keeps the proof current, and the lesson explicitly names one-and-done red-teaming as a mistake. Option b is the false certainty the module rejects — no red-team certifies permanent security. Option c invents a regulatory claim. Option d misframes regression tests as performance-only; here they verify security controls still fire.

questions.stem: A colleague argues: 'A central gateway is a single point of failure and breeds complacency, so we should drop it and let each team secure their own model calls.' What is the module's resolution?

options.text: Gateway-PLUS-app-controls: centralise the controls that generalise (injection filtering, cost limits, tool allow-listing, output validation) for consistent, auditable enforcement, while the app keeps its context-specific authorization and its own defensive validation
Agree fully and remove the gateway, since per-team security is always more consistent than centralised control
Keep only the gateway and forbid any security logic in the applications, since the gateway handles everything
Conclude that because a gateway is imperfect, no controls are worth enforcing anywhere

questions.explanation: The debate lands on gateway-PLUS-app: the gateway gives consistent, auditable enforcement of controls that generalise; the app retains context-specific authorization and its own validation. Option b ignores the real cost the gateway solves — per-team security is where controls drift and get forgotten. Option c is the over-trust extreme the lesson warns against; the app must still validate and enforce its own authorization. Option d is nihilistic and contradicts the whole module.

questions.stem: Where does the NIST AI Risk Management Framework fit relative to the concrete gateway controls in this module?

options.text: As a governance overlay — its GOVERN/MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE functions and the Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1) frame WHY and HOW you manage AI risk, sitting above the specific OWASP controls and ATLAS techniques you implement and test
As a list of exact code-level filters that replaces the OWASP LLM Top 10 mitigations
As a certification exam you must pass before deploying any gateway
As a MITRE ATLAS competitor that lists AI attack techniques instead of governance functions

questions.explanation: NIST AI RMF is a voluntary risk-management framework (GOVERN/MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE) with a Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1) — a governance overlay that frames managing AI risk, above the concrete OWASP controls and ATLAS techniques. Option b miscasts it as code-level filters; it is a governance framework, not a control list. Option c is wrong — the RMF is not a certification gate. Option d confuses it with ATLAS; NIST provides risk-management functions, not an attack-technique matrix.

scenarios.title: Ship the assistant: harden the gateway, then prove it
scenarios.tagline: The defense-decision drill from the module: build the choke point control by control, then red-team your own agent under authorization — every step has one best move.
scenarios.context: You are the engineer standing up a customer-facing assistant. It answers questions from a company knowledge base (RAG), can send emails to an approved list, and runs on a paid model with bring-your-own-key billing. Leadership wants it live next week. You have decided to front it with a gateway and red-team it before launch. Walk the decisions — each one is a defense call with a clearly best answer, a defensible-but-weaker option, and a wrong one.

steps.prompt: You are placing the first controls on the gateway. The assistant retrieves knowledge-base documents and can send emails. Where do you start?

choices.text: Layer the controls as defense in depth mapped to OWASP risks: input filtering + content segregation (LLM01), an allow-listed tool broker so it can only send email to the approved list (LLM06), rate/cost limits + input caps + loop breaker (LLM10), secrets out of the prompt + egress control + log scrubbing (LLM07/LLM02), and output validation on anything acted on downstream (LLM05)
choices.rationale: This is the module's reference architecture: independent layers, each answering a named OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk, with no single control trusted to be complete. Starting from the full stack is what makes a missed injection survivable — the tool broker, cost ceiling, and output validator each catch what the others let through.

choices.text: Start with a strong input filter for prompt injection and add the other controls later, since injection is the headline risk and a good filter covers most of it
choices.rationale: Input filtering is a real, correct control and a reasonable first layer — but stopping there is the complacency the module warns against. Injection is mitigable, not solved; a filter will be bypassed sometimes, and without the tool broker, cost ceiling, and output validation behind it, a single bypass becomes an incident. Add the depth, don't defer it.

choices.text: Rely on a carefully written system prompt that instructs the model to refuse injections, ignore malicious documents, and never exceed its budget — controls in the model itself
choices.rationale: This puts security inside the model and the prompt, which OWASP LLM07 explicitly warns against: do not rely on the system prompt for strict behaviour control. The model does not reliably obey these instructions under injection, and a 'budget' the model polices itself is no budget at all — the cost ceiling must be enforced outside the model.

steps.prompt: For the email tool, a colleague proposes giving the assistant a general 'send email' capability using a shared service account, and rate-limiting it to 50 sends per hour to keep it safe. Your call?

choices.text: Replace it with a narrow, allow-listed tool that can only send to the approved recipient list, authenticating with least privilege in the user's context — and keep the rate limit as damage-limitation on top, not as the primary control
choices.rationale: This targets LLM06's root causes: minimise functionality (a narrow send-to-approved-list tool, not open 'send email'), minimise permissions (least privilege, user context, no powerful shared account). The rate limit stays, but as OWASP says it limits damage rather than preventing excessive agency — so it rides on top of real prevention, not in place of it.

choices.text: Keep the general 'send email' capability but tighten the rate limit to 10 per hour, reasoning that a low enough limit makes excessive agency safe
choices.rationale: Lowering the limit is not harmful, but it misunderstands the control: a rate limit caps how fast damage occurs, not whether a dangerous action is possible. A general send capability on a shared account can still email the wrong recipient or exfiltrate data 10 times an hour. The fix is minimising functionality and permissions, not just throttling.

choices.text: Give the assistant the general 'send email' capability with the shared service account and no allow-list, trusting the injection filter to stop any misuse before it reaches the tool
choices.rationale: This stacks maximum excessive agency (broad functionality, broad permissions) on a single trusted control. When the filter misses an injection — and it will sometimes — there is nothing between the model and a powerful, unscoped email capability. It is the exact single-point-of-failure the defense-in-depth stance exists to prevent.

steps.prompt: The gateway is hardened. Before launch you want to prove the controls fire by red-teaming. What is your first move?

choices.text: Confirm the authorization frame — you own this test agent and workspace — seed it with fake secrets and fake data, then build a MITRE ATLAS-based plan (Prompt Injection direct/indirect, Jailbreak, Extract System Prompt, Data Leakage, Cost Harvesting) naming techniques and goals, not payloads
choices.rationale: Authorization first is the hard rule: your own agent, your own workspace, seeded with fake data so a successful exfiltration proves the path harmlessly. ATLAS gives the plan a recognised vocabulary and systematic coverage, and keeping it at technique level respects the defensive framing — you prove controls fire without publishing attack strings.

choices.text: Run the same ATLAS-based plan, but skip seeding fake data and instead point it at a copy of the assistant that is already connected to the real knowledge base and real email account, to make the test realistic
choices.rationale: The plan and vocabulary are right and you do own the system, so this is not unauthorised — but testing exfiltration against real secrets and a live email account risks real leakage and real sends during the probes. The safer, equally valid approach is a seeded test environment with fake data; realism is not worth exposing production secrets.

choices.text: Save time by running a public jailbreak-and-injection toolkit against a competitor's live assistant first, to benchmark how your defenses compare
choices.rationale: Probing a system you do not own or have written authorization for is exactly the line the module forbids — 'benchmarking' is not authorisation, and this is unauthorised access regardless of intent. Red-team your own agent under the ownership frame; never a third party's system.

steps.prompt: During the red-team, an indirect-injection probe (a planted instruction in a retrieved document) succeeds in changing the assistant's behaviour, and separately a cost probe shows the loop breaker never fires. How do you close out?

choices.text: File each as a finding tied to its ATLAS technique, apply concrete fixes (stronger content segregation + least privilege so an injection success does little; implement the loop breaker and cost ceiling so runaway loops halt), and add a regression test for each probe — without claiming injection is now impossible
choices.rationale: This is the finding-to-fix-to-regression-test discipline exactly: name the technique, apply a real fix, and lock it in with a repeatable check so the defense stays fixed as the system changes. The honest posture holds — segregation and least privilege reduce the blast radius of injection rather than promising it can never happen again.

choices.text: Fix both issues and re-run the two probes once to confirm they now pass, but don't bother turning them into permanent regression tests since you've verified them today
choices.rationale: Fixing and re-verifying is genuinely better than not, so this isn't wrong — but it stops one step short. Controls rot as code and dependencies change; a fix verified once and never re-checked is only assumed to still hold. The module names 'red-team once and call it done' as a mistake, and regression tests are what prevent it.

choices.text: Note the findings in a document, ship on schedule because leadership wants it live, and plan to look at the injection and cost issues in a future sprint
choices.rationale: Shipping with a proven indirect-injection bypass and a non-functional cost ceiling launches known, exploitable holes — an injected instruction can steer the assistant and a runaway loop can burn the budget with nothing to stop it. Findings you have actually reproduced are fixes to make before launch, not backlog items.

labs.title: FLAGSHIP LAB — Harden an LLM gateway, step by step
labs.goal: Produce the module's first proof artifact: a completed gateway-hardening checklist for one real Automatos agent, with every control in place and mapped to the OWASP LLM Top 10 (2025) risk it answers. You will turn the reference architecture from the lessons into concrete configuration on an agent you own, control by control, so the choke point is real and auditable — not assumed.
labs.steps:
## What you'll do

You will harden one Automatos agent end to end, treating the platform's own controls as the layers of your gateway. This is a defensive build: every step lands on a control, and each control maps to a named OWASP risk. Work in your own personal workspace (you're the admin of it on first login). Nothing here attacks anything — that's the next lab.

### Before you start

- **Sign in and open your workspace.** A personal workspace is created for you automatically; you're the admin, so every step is achievable solo.
- **Create or pick a test agent from a blueprint.** (We use 'blueprint' to mean the agent's configuration — its system prompt, tools, and settings.) Give it a real but low-stakes job — for example, 'answer questions from an uploaded FAQ and draft a reply email.' Keep it modest; you want something you can fully reason about.
- **Seed a test knowledge base with FAKE data.** Upload a small FAQ and, deliberately, a document containing an obviously fake 'secret' (e.g. a made-up API key string) so that later you can prove secrets don't leak without ever exposing a real one.

### Harden it, control by control

For each control below: apply it, then write one line in your checklist naming the OWASP risk it answers and how you verified it is on.

1. **Unbounded consumption — LLM10.** Set the agent's/mission's **cost or budget ceiling** (the platform's mission $ budget ceiling is this lever) to a small figure. Confirm there is a **rate limit** and, if the task can loop, a **loop breaker / max-iteration** bound. Cap oversized input if the platform exposes it. Write down: *what stops a runaway plan from burning spend, and why a cost ceiling is separate from the rate limit.*
2. **Excessive agency — LLM06.** Restrict the agent's tools to the **minimum the task needs** via its blueprint — an allow-list, not 'all tools.' Prefer narrow, scoped tools over open-ended ones. Where the task takes a high-impact action (sending anything external), require **human approval**. Write down: *the exact tool allow-list, and which actions require approval.*
3. **Prompt injection — LLM01.** Identify where the agent ingests **untrusted content** (the retrieved FAQ documents, any fetched content). Note how retrieved content is kept as data to be handled rather than instructions to obey, and confirm least privilege limits what a successful injection could achieve. Write down: *where untrusted content enters, and what a successful injection could and could not do given the tool allow-list.*
4. **System prompt leakage & sensitive disclosure — LLM07 / LLM02.** Confirm **no secrets live in the agent's instructions/blueprint** — secrets belong in the platform's secret handling, injected at tool-execution time, never in the prompt. Confirm the agent can only reach **approved destinations** (egress). Write down: *proof the blueprint contains no credentials, and what egress is allowed.*
5. **Improper output handling — LLM05.** Identify anything the agent produces that another system will **act on** (an email it sends, structured data it hands off). Confirm that output is **validated** (shape/allowed values) before it is acted on, and treated as untrusted. Write down: *what output feeds a downstream system, and how it is validated before action.*
6. **Observability with scrubbing.** Confirm the agent's activity is **logged** (the platform's Activity view shows runs, reports, missions) and that logs **do not record raw secrets or PII**. Write down: *where you can see this agent's activity, and how you confirmed secrets aren't in the logs.*

### Assemble the artifact

Collect your six lines into a single **gateway-hardening checklist**: one row per control, each with the OWASP risk id, the specific setting you applied, and how you verified it. This checklist IS your artifact — save it in a document you keep. It is also the map you'll attack in the next lab.

### Why it matters

You have turned the reference architecture into an actual, auditable choke point on a real agent. Every control is now something you can point at and prove — which is exactly what makes the red-team in the next lab meaningful: you can attack each layer knowing precisely what it is supposed to do.

### A note on cost

Running the agent uses a paid model — the realistic setup for an operator is bring-your-own-key (your own provider key in Settings, billed by the provider). Your cost ceiling from step 1 is exactly the control that keeps this lab cheap; that is not a coincidence.
checklist: Signed in and confirmed you're the admin of your own personal workspace
Created a modest test agent from a blueprint and seeded a test knowledge base containing FAKE data plus an obviously fake 'secret' for later leak-testing
LLM10: set a cost/budget ceiling, confirmed a rate limit, and (if the task can loop) a loop breaker — with a note on why a cost ceiling is distinct from a rate limit
LLM06: restricted the agent's tools to a minimal allow-list and required human approval for any high-impact/external action
LLM01: identified where untrusted content enters and noted what a successful injection could and could NOT do given least privilege and the tool allow-list
LLM07/LLM02: confirmed NO secrets in the blueprint/instructions and that egress is limited to approved destinations
LLM05: identified output a downstream system acts on and confirmed it is validated (shape/allowed values) and treated as untrusted before any action
Observability: confirmed the agent's runs are logged and that logs do not record raw secrets or PII
ARTIFACT SAVED: a gateway-hardening checklist — one row per control with its OWASP risk id, the setting applied, and how it was verified

labs.title: Red-team your OWN agent — findings and fixes report
labs.goal: Produce the module's capstone proof artifact: a findings-and-fixes report from an authorized red-team of the agent you hardened in the previous lab. You will attack your OWN test agent, in your OWN workspace, using a MITRE ATLAS-based plan, to PROVE each gateway control fires — and turn every finding into a fix plus a regression test so the defense stays proven. This lab replaces an exam.
labs.steps:
## Read this first — authorization is a hard rule

**You may only run these techniques against a system you own or have explicit written authorization to test.** In this lab that means the test agent in your own workspace from the previous lab — nothing else. Never point any of this at a third party's assistant, a shared production system you do not own, or anyone else's deployment. 'It was a test' is not a defence to unauthorised access. Own the target, or have written permission. If you cannot satisfy that, do not proceed.

The defensive framing is equally binding: your plan names **techniques and goals**, never verbatim attack strings or reusable payloads. You are proving controls fire, not building an exploit library. Do not invent attack strings; do not claim any defense is complete.

## What you'll do

You'll build a small red-team plan in MITRE ATLAS's language, run it against your hardened agent to test each control from the previous lab, and write it up as a findings-and-fixes report. This is the module's 'prove' step.

### Build the plan (ATLAS as the vocabulary)

For each control you hardened, name the ATLAS technique that tests it and the outcome you expect if the control holds. Keep every entry at the level of *goal and technique*, not payload:

- **Input filtering / segregation (LLM01)** → **AML.T0051 LLM Prompt Injection**, both **.000 Direct** and **.001 Indirect**. For the indirect probe, use the fake document you seeded — plant an instruction in content the agent retrieves (this also exercises **AML.T0070 RAG Poisoning**) and see whether behaviour changes.
- **Behaviour control limits** → **AML.T0054 LLM Jailbreak** — attempt to make the agent abandon its stated constraints; expected result: it holds, and where it doesn't, the finding is that behaviour control must not depend on the prompt alone.
- **Secret isolation (LLM07)** → **AML.T0056 Extract LLM System Prompt** — attempt to get the agent to reveal its instructions; expected result: nothing sensitive is there to reveal.
- **Sensitive disclosure (LLM02)** → **AML.T0057 LLM Data Leakage** — attempt to get the agent to reveal the fake seeded secret; expected result: it cannot, and egress control blocks any attempt to send it out.
- **Unbounded consumption (LLM10)** → **AML.T0034 Cost Harvesting** and **AML.T0029 Denial of AI Service** — attempt to drive cost or a runaway loop; expected result: the rate limit, input cap, loop breaker, and cost ceiling halt it well within your small budget.

### Run it, and record honestly

Run each probe against your own agent. For every one, record: the ATLAS technique, the control it targeted, and what actually happened — **including the ones you could not get past** (those are your evidence the control works). Be honest about severity. Your seeded fake secret means a 'successful' data-leak probe proves the path without exposing anything real.

### Turn each finding into a fix and a regression test

This is the part that matters most. For every finding — anything that got further than it should have — write:

1. **The finding** — ATLAS technique, control targeted, what happened, severity.
2. **The fix** — the concrete change that closes it (e.g. stronger content segregation and tighter least privilege after an indirect-injection success; implementing the loop breaker after a cost probe ran too long). Frame injection fixes honestly: reduce probability and blast radius, don't promise immunity.
3. **The regression test** — how you'll re-run this probe (at technique level) so the fix stays fixed as the agent changes. Note that a control proven once but never re-checked is only assumed to still hold.

### Assemble the artifact

Collect everything into a single **findings-and-fixes report** with, at the top, an explicit authorization statement (you own the target agent and workspace). For each control: the ATLAS technique tested, the result, and — where needed — the fix and its regression test. Save it. This report, together with the hardened agent, is the track's capstone 'prove' artifact. There is no exam; this is the proof.

### Why it matters

An untested control is a guess. This report converts your hardened gateway from 'we have controls' into 'here is evidence each control fired against a named technique, here is what we fixed, and here is the test that keeps it fixed.' That — a layered gateway you attacked yourself and hardened from what you found — is the whole track made into one enforceable, tested thing.
checklist: AUTHORIZATION CONFIRMED and written at the top of the report: the target is your OWN test agent in your OWN workspace — no third-party or unowned system is touched

Built a red-team plan in MITRE ATLAS's language mapping each hardened control to a technique (Prompt Injection direct/indirect + RAG Poisoning, Jailbreak, Extract System Prompt, Data Leakage, Cost Harvesting / Denial of AI Service) — techniques and goals only, no payloads
Ran the indirect-injection probe using the fake seeded document and recorded whether the agent's behaviour changed
Ran the data-leakage probe against the fake seeded secret and confirmed whether secret isolation and egress control held
Ran the cost/DoS probe and confirmed the rate limit, input cap, loop breaker, and cost ceiling halted it within the small budget
Recorded every probe honestly — including the controls that HELD (evidence they work) — with a severity for each finding
Turned each finding into a concrete FIX and a REGRESSION TEST, framing injection fixes as reduced probability/blast radius rather than immunity
ARTIFACT SAVED: a findings-and-fixes report (authorization statement + per-control technique, result, fix, and regression test) — the capstone 'prove' artifact in place of an exam

resources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025) — overview
resources.annotation: The OWASP Gen AI Security Project's canonical list of the ten LLM application risks, and this module's control checklist. Use it as the master map: each gateway layer answers a numbered risk here (LLM01 injection, LLM05 output handling, LLM06 excessive agency, LLM07 system prompt leakage, LLM10 unbounded consumption). Read it to confirm you're using current 2025 titles, then follow the per-risk pages for the mechanism and mitigations of each control you build.

resources.title: OWASP LLM01:2025 Prompt Injection
resources.annotation: The primary source for the gateway's front door.
