Read it for OWASP's own definition ('user prompts alter the LLM's behavior in unintended ways,' content need not be human-visible), the direct-vs-indirect distinction that decides where you place the control, and the mitigation list you implement: constrain behaviour, segregate external content, filter input/output, enforce least privilege, require human approval for high-risk actions, and adversarially test. Note it never claims injection is solved — the honest posture this module keeps.

resources.title: OWASP LLM10:2025 Unbounded Consumption
resources.annotation: The source for the module's most expensive-to-get-wrong layer. Read it for how the 2025 title absorbed 'Model Denial of Service' and now centres cost and resource exhaustion (denial of service, economic loss, model theft), the examples (Variable-Length Input Flood, Denial of Wallet, Resource-Intensive Queries, Model Extraction via API), and the mitigations that become gateway controls: input validation and size caps, rate limiting, timeouts and throttling, resource/queue limits, access controls, and monitoring. This is where the cost-ceiling-vs-rate-limit distinction comes from.

resources.title: OWASP LLM06:2025 Excessive Agency
resources.annotation: The source for the allow-listed tool broker. Read it for the three root causes (excessive functionality, excessive permissions, excessive autonomy) and the prevention list: minimise extensions and functionality, avoid open-ended extensions, minimise permissions (least privilege, execute in user context), require user approval for high-impact actions, and complete mediation downstream. Critical exam point stated here: rate limiting and monitoring LIMIT DAMAGE but do NOT prevent excessive agency.

resources.title: OWASP LLM05:2025 Improper Output Handling
resources.annotation: The source for the gateway's exit door and structured output validation. Read it for OWASP's exact definition — insufficient validation/sanitisation of model output BEFORE it passes downstream — the distinction from overreliance, the examples (RCE via shell, XSS via rendered HTML/Markdown, SQL injection, path traversal), and the defenses: treat output as untrusted (zero-trust), context-specific encoding, parameterised queries/prepared statements, CSP, and validation against a schema. This is the layer that contains an injection the front door missed.

resources.title: OWASP LLM07:2025 System Prompt Leakage
resources.annotation: The source for secret isolation, and for the module's sharpest lesson: disclosure of the system prompt is not the primary risk — the underlying failure of putting secrets there and relying on the prompt for security is. Read it for the guidance that credentials and connection strings must not live in the system prompt, and the mitigations: separate sensitive data out, do not rely on system prompts for strict behaviour control, and implement guardrails independently of the LLM. Pair with LLM02 for the broader sensitive-disclosure controls.

resources.title: OWASP LLM02:2025 Sensitive Information Disclosure
resources.annotation: The companion to LLM07 for the gateway's secret and PII controls. Read it for the risk that PII, credentials, and proprietary data leak through model output, and the mitigations that shape the gateway: data sanitisation, input validation to filter sensitive inputs, least-privilege access to sensitive data, and limiting the model's access to external data sources. This underpins the log-scrubbing and egress-control decisions in the hardening lab.

resources.title: MITRE ATLAS — Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems
resources.annotation: The technique language for the red-team lab. MITRE ATLAS is a living knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques against AI systems, modelled on MITRE ATT&CK. Use it to name your red-team plan: LLM Prompt Injection (AML.T0051, with Direct .000 and Indirect .001 sub-techniques), LLM Jailbreak (AML.T0054), RAG Poisoning (AML.T0070), Extract LLM System Prompt (AML.T0056), LLM Data Leakage (AML.T0057), Cost Harvesting (AML.T0034), and Denial of AI Service (AML.T0029). Naming findings in ATLAS terms makes them recognisable and ensures systematic coverage — use it for the vocabulary and goals, never as a payload source.

resources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0) + Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1)
resources.annotation: The governance overlay above the concrete controls. The NIST AI RMF is a voluntary framework organised around four functions — GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE — for managing AI risk across a system's life. Its companion Generative AI Profile (NIST-AI-600-1, released 26 July 2024) identifies risks unique to or exacerbated by generative AI and proposes actions to manage them. Read it to see where the OWASP controls and ATLAS red-teaming sit inside a formal risk-management and governance process; it hands off to the AIGP governance track.

videos.title: M5 · The LLM gateway — one choke point, defense in depth (build the reference architecture, then red-team it)
M5 · Does a central gateway make you safer, or is it a false sense of security? (debate)
